summary: Gruber's comprehensive review of the iPhone 16 lineup centers on Camera Control as the most significant hardware innovation — a dedicated photography button that represents Apple's boldest hardware addition in 18 years. He traces the philosophical lineage from Jobs's original elimination of hardware buttons to this purposeful addition of one, arguing the iPhone has always been a camera first and phone second. He finds Camera Control genuinely useful despite frustrations with its placement and lock-screen behavior, and praises the new non-destructive Photographic Styles as 'RAW for the rest of us.' The review's sharpest critique targets Apple's marketing emphasis on Apple Intelligence, which Gruber dismisses as riding the AI hype wave rather than highlighting the lineup's true strengths in photography and value.
key_points: Camera Control is the first hardware button Apple has dedicated to a single application in 18 iPhone generations, representing a radical commitment to photography over all other functions
The new non-destructive Photographic Styles effectively deliver 'RAW for the rest of us' — letting casual photographers adjust processing after the fact without the workflow burden of shooting RAW
The regular iPhone 16 models offer exceptional value this year, gaining both the Action button and Camera Control, making the $200 Pro premium harder to justify for non-professionals
Apple Intelligence features currently available are incremental improvements dressed up in tentpole marketing — normal yearly feature additions amplified by the industry's AI hype wave
Camera Control's placement is a compromise between landscape and portrait shooting orientations, and its lock-screen launch behavior creates frustrating delays for spontaneous photography
money_quotes: If you compared an iPhone to an iPod Touch, there was nothing on the iPhone hardware that indicated it was any more of a phone than the iPod Touch, which not only wasn't a phone but didn't even offer cellular networking.
Apple's Camera app is one of the best — and best-designed — pieces of software the world has ever seen. It's arguably the most-copied interface the world has ever seen, too.
It takes a while to get used to — I feel like I'm still getting used to it — but it already feels like something I wouldn't want to do without.

I've always felt a little guilty about the fact that I'm too lazy to shoot RAW. This next-generation Photographic Styles feature in the iPhone 16 lineup might assuage, I suspect, the remaining vestiges of that guilt.
Apple's just riding — and now, through the impressive might of its own advertising and marketing, contributing to — the AI hype wave, and I find that a little eye-roll inducing.

Every phone on the market will soon be able to generate impersonal saccharine passages of text and uncanny-valley images via LLMs. Only Apple has the talent and passion to create something as innovative and genuinely useful as Camera Control.

If the iPhone 16 lineup was 'built from the ground up' with a purpose in mind, it's to serve as the best prosumer cameras ever made. Not to create cartoon images of a dog blowing out candles on a birthday cake.
The non-pro iPhone 16 and 16 Plus arguably offer the best value-per-dollar of any iPhones Apple has ever made. This emphasis on Apple Intelligence distracts from that.
key_insight: The iPhone 16's true innovation is Camera Control — a bold hardware dedication to photography that proves Apple's real strengths, while the marketed tentpole of Apple Intelligence is just incremental features amplified by industry AI hype.
